site.name: Share Capsule Tips
site.tagline: One validated life hack every day
site.method: Tips rotate deterministically by local calendar date. Every tip must include an authoritative source and documented real-world evidence before publication.

dailyNewsSelections.headline: Extreme heat warnings, record-hot nights, wildfire danger and smoke affect large parts of the United States
dailyNewsSelections.explanation: Selected because unusually hot nights reduce the body's opportunity to recover, while heat, wildfire and smoke increase risks for older adults, children, outdoor workers and people without reliable cooling.

dailyNewsSelections.headline: Global crises, AI-enhanced manipulation and fast-moving social posts increase the need to verify urgent information before forwarding it
dailyNewsSelections.explanation: Selected because one careful pause can reduce harm during conflicts, disasters, health scares, scams and other fast-moving events in every country.

dailyNewsSelections.headline: UAE condemns Iran's 'brazen' attack on tankers as US launches fresh strikes
dailyNewsSelections.explanation: Selected from authenticated tip content because its themes best matched the previous 24 hours of news.

tips.title: Pause before forwarding urgent information
tips.action: Before sharing a frightening, exciting or urgent message, stop briefly and verify its source, date and context with at least one independent reliable source.
steps: Stop when a message creates a strong emotional reaction or demands immediate sharing.
Open the original source instead of trusting a screenshot, cropped clip or forwarded caption.
Check the publication date, location and whether an old event is being presented as new.
Confirm the central claim through another established news outlet, official agency or fact-checking organization before forwarding it.
tips.whyItWorks: A short pause interrupts impulsive sharing and creates time to detect altered images, recycled stories, fake urgency and missing context before they spread to family or community groups.
tips.sourceOrganization: UNESCO media and information literacy research
tips.sourceTitle: UNESCO warning on the need for fact-checking skills among online content creators
tips.validationNote: UNESCO research across creators in 45 countries found widespread gaps in verification practices and emphasized checking accuracy and using credible sources before publishing or sharing information.
tips.evidenceTitle: Community fact-checking reduced the spread of misleading social posts
tips.evidenceSummary: A large-scale study of more than 237,000 fact-checked social-media cascades found that visible community fact checks substantially reduced continued sharing, supporting the value of verification before amplification.

tips.title: Keep the cold in during a power outage
tips.action: Keep refrigerator and freezer doors closed, and note when the outage started.
steps: Avoid opening the refrigerator or freezer unless essential.
A closed refrigerator keeps food safe for up to 4 hours.
A full closed freezer holds a safe temperature for about 48 hours; a half-full freezer for about 24 hours.
Never taste food to decide whether it is safe.
tips.whyItWorks: Every door opening releases cold air. Preserving the cold delays the time food spends above safe temperatures.
tips.sourceOrganization: FoodSafety.gov — U.S. Department of Health and Human Services
tips.validationNote: FoodSafety.gov states that a refrigerator keeps food safe for up to 4 hours during an outage when kept closed, and a full freezer for approximately 48 hours or a half-full freezer for 24 hours.
tips.evidenceTitle: 2021 Texas Severe Winter Storm — FEMA disaster 4586
tips.evidenceSummary: The FEMA-declared February 2021 Texas winter storm produced extended power interruptions, illustrating why tracking outage time and preserving refrigerator temperature matters during real emergencies.

tips.title: Cool down, hydrate and check on people during extreme heat
tips.action: Move to air conditioning or a cooler place, drink water regularly, reduce strenuous activity and check older adults, children and people without reliable cooling.
steps: Use an air-conditioned location when possible.
Drink water regularly instead of waiting until you feel thirsty.
Move outdoor activity to cooler hours and take more breaks.
Check people at higher risk and never leave children or pets in a parked vehicle.
tips.whyItWorks: Heat illness develops when the body cannot shed heat. Cooling, hydration, rest and early attention to symptoms reduce the chance that heat exhaustion progresses to heat stroke.
tips.sourceOrganization: U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
tips.validationNote: CDC guidance emphasizes staying cool, staying hydrated, recognizing symptoms and checking people at greater risk during hot weather.
tips.evidenceTitle: Record-warm nights during the July 2026 U.S. heat wave
tips.evidenceSummary: Current reporting describes unusually hot days and nights across much of the United States, increasing health risks because the body has less opportunity to cool and recover overnight.

tips.title: Run a portable generator outside — at least 20 feet away
tips.action: Place the generator outdoors at least 20 feet from the home, with exhaust facing away from doors, windows and vents.
steps: Never run a generator inside a home, basement, shed or garage — even with doors open.
Position it at least 20 feet from the building.
Point the exhaust away from the home.
Keep working carbon-monoxide alarms on every level and outside sleeping areas.
tips.whyItWorks: Portable generators can produce extremely high levels of invisible, odorless carbon monoxide that can enter a building through openings.
tips.validationNote: CPSC explicitly says generators must be used outside only, at least 20 feet from homes, with exhaust facing away.
tips.evidenceTitle: More than 100 U.S. deaths each year are linked to portable generators
tips.evidenceSummary: CPSC reports more than 200 annual accidental non-fire carbon-monoxide deaths associated with consumer products, with more than 100 linked to portable generators.

tips.title: Never test a flooded road with your vehicle
tips.action: Turn around instead of driving or walking into floodwater, even when the water looks shallow.
steps: Do not drive around a road barrier.
Do not assume the road still exists beneath the water.
Choose another route or wait for officials to reopen the road.
At night, treat any water-covered roadway as impassable.
tips.whyItWorks: Moving water can hide a washed-out roadway and generate enough force to move vehicles surprisingly quickly.
tips.validationNote: NWS states that 12 inches of rushing water can carry away most cars and 2 feet can carry away SUVs and trucks.
tips.evidenceTitle: Over half of flood-related drownings occur in vehicles
tips.evidenceSummary: The National Weather Service cites CDC data showing that more than half of flood-related drownings occur when a vehicle is driven into hazardous floodwater.

tips.title: Keep a lid beside the pan before you start cooking
tips.action: Before heating oil or cooking on the stovetop, place a matching metal lid or baking sheet within reach.
steps: Stay near the pan while the burner is on.
If smoke appears or grease begins to boil, turn the burner off.
If the pan catches fire and it is safe to act, cover it with the lid or baking sheet to smother the flames.
Turn pot handles toward the back of the stove.
tips.whyItWorks: Covering a contained pan fire blocks oxygen, while having the lid ready avoids searching for it during an emergency.
tips.validationNote: USFA advises keeping a pan lid or baking sheet nearby and using it to cover a pan that catches fire.
tips.evidenceTitle: U.S. fire departments responded to about 170,000 home cooking fires in 2021
tips.evidenceSummary: USFA reports that those fires caused an estimated 135 deaths, 3,000 injuries and more than $494 million in property loss; unattended equipment was the leading ignition factor in nonconfined cooking fires.

tips.title: Close your bedroom door before sleeping
tips.action: Make closing bedroom doors part of the nightly routine after checking that smoke and carbon-monoxide alarms are working.
steps: Close bedroom doors before going to sleep.
Keep smoke alarms working and audible inside sleeping areas.
Make sure everyone knows two ways out and the household meeting place.
If an alarm sounds, follow the escape plan immediately.
tips.whyItWorks: A closed door can slow the movement of heat, smoke and toxic gases, preserving more survivable conditions for longer.
tips.sourceOrganization: UL Research Institutes — Fire Safety Research Institute
tips.validationNote: FSRI's recommendation follows more than a decade of full-scale fire research on the effect of open and closed doors.
tips.evidenceTitle: Full-scale home-fire experiments measured dramatic room differences
tips.evidenceSummary: FSRI reports average temperatures below 100°F in closed-door rooms versus more than 1,000°F in open-door rooms, with carbon monoxide around 100 ppm closed versus about 10,000 ppm open in the cited tests.

tips.title: At the first jolt: Drop, Cover and Hold On
tips.action: Immediately get low, protect your head and neck, and hold on to sturdy shelter until shaking stops.
steps: Drop onto your hands and knees so you are less likely to be knocked down.
Cover your head and neck; crawl under a sturdy table if one is nearby.
Hold on to the shelter and be ready to move with it.
Do not run outside or stand in a doorway during the shaking.
tips.whyItWorks: Most earthquake injuries in modern U.S. buildings come from falling, flying or shifting objects rather than total building collapse.
tips.sourceOrganization: Great ShakeOut Earthquake Drills — Statewide California Earthquake Center
tips.validationNote: Federal, state and local emergency-management experts cited by ShakeOut agree that Drop, Cover and Hold On is the appropriate protective action in most situations.
tips.evidenceTitle: A real magnitude 4.2 Delta earthquake triggered California's ShakeAlert in October 2023
tips.evidenceSummary: The earthquake occurred one day before the annual ShakeOut drill, underscoring the need to practice the protective action before an unexpected alert or shaking begins.
